titles: Abu al-Bashar · Safi Allah (the Chosen of God) · Khalifat Allah
school: First prophet and first human being in Islamic cosmology; taught the names of all things; recipient of divine revelation; ancestor of all humanity

books.title: Qisas al-Anbiya (Ibn Kathir)
books.note: Primary source for Adam's story in Islamic tradition

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Baqara (2:30–39)
books.note: The creation and descent of Adam

primaryTitle: First Prophet after Adam — Keeper of the Scrolls
titles: Shith · Seth · Hibat Allah (Gift of God)
school: Son of Adam; first prophet appointed after Adam's death in Islamic tradition; received divine scrolls (suhuf) according to hadith. Al-Tabari records him as the guardian of Adam's knowledge. His lineage runs through Idris to Nuh.
Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adam and Idris in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adam and Idris in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

titles: Idris · identified with Enoch (Khanukh) · Hermes Trismegistus in some traditions
school: Quranic prophet raised to a high station; associated in Islamic tradition with the origins of writing, astronomy, and crafts; praised in the Quran as truthful and a prophet raised to a high place

books.title: Quran — Sura Maryam (19:56–57)
books.note: Quranic mention of Idris raised to a lofty station

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Idris and Nuh in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Idris and Nuh in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

primaryTitle: Shaykh al-Anbiya — Elder of the Prophets
titles: Nuh · Shaykh al-Anbiya · Abd al-Shakur (the Grateful Servant)
school: One of the five greatest prophets (Ulu al-Azm); preached for 950 years; builder of the Ark; ancestor of post-flood humanity; an entire Quranic sura bears his name

books.title: Quran — Sura Nuh (71)
books.note: The full sura dedicated to Nuh's mission

primaryTitle: Son of Nuh — Ancestor of the Semitic Peoples
titles: Sam · Shem · eldest son of Nuh
school: Eldest son of Nuh who boarded the Ark; forefather of the Semitic peoples. Al-Tabari traces the full chain of Semitic patriarchs from Sam through to Ibrahim. Ibrahim, Ismail, and Prophet Muhammad all descend through Sam.
Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Nuh and Ibrahim in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

primaryTitle: Father of Ibrahim — Named in the Quran
titles: Azar · Terah (biblical parallel) · father of Ibrahim
school: Father of Ibrahim; the only father of a prophet named directly in the Quran (6:74). An idol-worshipper whom Ibrahim tried to guide to monotheism; his refusal became a defining trial of Ibrahim's mission. Mentioned four times in the Quran.

books.title: Quran — Sura al-An'am (6:74)
books.note: Ibrahim's address to his father Azar about idol worship

primaryTitle: Khalil Allah — Friend of God, Father of Prophets
city: Ur of the Chaldees / Canaan / Mecca
titles: Khalil Allah · Abu al-Anbiya · Hanif · Ibrahim al-Khalil
school: One of the five greatest prophets (Ulu al-Azm); father of monotheism; builder of the Ka'ba with Ismail; progenitor of both the Arab (through Ismail) and Israelite (through Ishaq) prophetic lines; model of complete submission to God

books.title: Quran — Sura Ibrahim (14)
books.note: The sura named for Ibrahim, containing his supplications

primaryTitle: Dhabih Allah — The Sacrifice of God, Father of the Arabs
titles: Ismail · Dhabih Allah · Abu al-'Arab · Sayyid al-'Arab
school: First-born son of Ibrahim; helped build the Ka'ba in Mecca; ancestor of the Arab peoples and of Prophet Muhammad; known as Dhabih Allah for his willingness to be sacrificed; a prophet sent to the people of ancient Arabia

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Saffat (37:101–113)
books.note: The account of the sacrifice of Ismail

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Ismail and Adnan in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

primaryTitle: Accepted Progenitor of the Northern Arabs — The Reliable Ancestor
titles: Adnan · progenitor of the Adnanite Arabs · ancestor of Prophet Muhammad
school: The furthest point in Muhammad's lineage that classical Muslim scholars consider historically reliable. Ibn Ishaq explicitly instructed scholars not to go beyond Adnan in claiming certainty, as the chain between Ismail and Adnan spans many unverifiable generations. Universally accepted ancestor of the northern (Adnanite) Arab tribes.
Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

primaryTitle: Eponymous Ancestor of the Quraysh Tribe
full: Fihr ibn Mālik ibn al-Nadr al-Qurashī
titles: Fihr · Quraysh (the tribe takes its name from him) · ancestor of Prophet Muhammad
school: The ancestor after whom the Quraysh tribe is named in the dominant genealogical tradition. The Quraysh were the guardians of the Ka'ba and the noblest tribe in Arabia. All early caliphs and the Prophet himself descended from Fihr. The name 'Quraysh' derives from the Arabic root meaning to gather or concentrate — a reference to Fihr's role consolidating his people.
Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

primaryTitle: Reunifier of the Quraysh — Founder of Meccan Governance
full: Qusayy ibn Kilāb ibn Murra al-Qurashī
titles: Qusayy · Mujammi' (the Gatherer) · great-great-grandfather of Muhammad
school: First historically well-attested ancestor of Muhammad. He seized control of Mecca from the Khuza'a tribe and united the Quraysh clans under his leadership. He built the Dar al-Nadwa (the council house of Mecca), established the custodianship of the Ka'ba in Quraysh, and created the civic offices of Mecca that endured until the Islamic era. Ibn Ishaq calls him the first great leader of the Quraysh.

books.title: Sirat Rasul Allah (Ibn Ishaq / Ibn Hisham)
books.note: Account of Qusayy's conquest of Mecca and establishment of Quraysh rule

school: Listed in the prophetic lineage between Adnan and Prophet Muhammad in Ibn Ishaq's Sira and al-Tabari's Tarikh. No independent biographical record survives beyond the genealogical chain.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Primary Islamic source for pre-Islamic genealogical chains

primaryTitle: Founder of Banu Hashim — Feeder of the Pilgrims
titles: Hashim · 'Amr al-'Ula · founder of the Hashimite clan · died in Gaza on trade
school: Great-grandfather of Prophet Muhammad and founder of the Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh. He established the two great Meccan trade caravans — the winter journey to Yemen and summer journey to Syria — referenced directly in Sura Quraysh (106). He fed pilgrims during the Hajj, a role so celebrated that his name Hashim means 'the one who crumbles bread'. He died in Gaza during a trading journey to Syria.

books.title: Quran — Sura Quraysh (106)
books.note: The winter and summer caravans established by Hashim ibn Abd Manaf

primaryTitle: Grandfather of the Prophet — Keeper of the Zamzam Well
full: Shayba ibn Hāshim, known as 'Abd al-Muṭṭalib al-Hāshimī
classif: Tribal Chief / Guardian of the Prophet
titles: Abd al-Muttalib · Shayba al-Hamd · guardian of the Ka'ba · raised the Prophet Muhammad
school: Grandfather and guardian of Prophet Muhammad. He rediscovered and redug the Zamzam well after receiving a divine dream directing him to its location. He raised Muhammad after the deaths of his father Abdullah and mother Amina. He led the Quraysh during Abraha's invasion — the Year of the Elephant (570 CE) in which the Ka'ba was miraculously protected. He died when Muhammad was approximately eight years old.

books.title: Tarikh al-Tabari
books.note: Account of Abd al-Muttalib, the Zamzam well, and the Year of the Elephant

titles: Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib · father of Prophet Muhammad
school: Father of Prophet Muhammad. He married Amina bint Wahb of the Banu Zuhra. He died in Medina during or returning from a trading journey to Syria, either shortly before or just after Muhammad's birth, making the Prophet an orphan from the start. The Quran references this: 'Did He not find you an orphan and shelter you?' (93:6).

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Duha (93:6)
books.note: God's reminder to Muhammad of his orphanhood

primaryTitle: Prophet of the 'Ad — Messenger to Ancient Arabia
titles: Hud · Prophet of the 'Ad people · mentioned 7 times in the Quran
school: Sent to the 'Ad people of ancient Arabia; preached monotheism against idol worship; his people were destroyed by a violent wind after rejecting him; a Quranic sura bears his name

books.title: Quran — Sura Hud (11)
books.note: Contains the full account of Hud and the 'Ad

primaryTitle: Prophet of Thamud — Messenger of the Rock-Hewn City
titles: Salih · Prophet of Thamud · associated with the miraculous she-camel
school: Sent to the Thamud people who carved their homes from rock at Al-Hijr (modern Mada'in Salih in Saudi Arabia); the miraculous she-camel was his sign; his people were destroyed by a thunderbolt after killing the camel

books.title: Quran — Sura al-A'raf (7:73–79)
books.note: The story of Salih and Thamud

primaryTitle: Prophet of Sodom — Messenger to the Cities of the Plain
titles: Lut · nephew of Ibrahim · prophet to the people of Sodom
school: Nephew of Ibrahim; sent to the people of Sodom (Qawm Lut); preached against their moral corruption; his people were destroyed; one of the most frequently mentioned prophets in the Quran

books.title: Quran — Sura Hud (11:77–83)
books.note: The full account of Lut and the destruction of his people

primaryTitle: Prophet of the Israelites — Second Son of Ibrahim
titles: Ishaq · Isaac · son of Ibrahim and Sarah · father of Yaqub
school: Son of Ibrahim and Sarah; father of Yaqub and grandfather of Yusuf; progenitor of the Israelite prophetic line; mentioned 17 times in the Quran; blessed by God as a prophet from birth

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Anbiya (21:72)
books.note: Mention of Ishaq as a gift to Ibrahim

primaryTitle: Isra'il — Father of the Twelve Tribes
titles: Yaqub · Israel (Isra'il) · son of Ishaq · father of the twelve tribes
school: Son of Ishaq, grandson of Ibrahim; given the name Isra'il; father of the twelve sons who became the progenitors of the twelve tribes of Israel, including Yusuf; prominent in the account of Yusuf in Sura Yusuf

books.title: Quran — Sura Yusuf (12)
books.note: Yaqub features prominently in the story of his son Yusuf

primaryTitle: Ahsan al-Qasas — The Most Beautiful of Stories
full: Yūsuf ibn Ya'qūb ibn Isḥāq ibn Ibrāhīm
titles: Yusuf · Joseph the Beautiful · Siddiq (the Truthful) · Vizier of Egypt
school: Son of Yaqub; his life story is described in the Quran as 'the most beautiful of stories'; sold into slavery by his brothers, rose to become Vizier of Egypt; known for his beauty, patience, wisdom, and ability to interpret dreams; a model of forgiveness

books.title: Quran — Sura Yusuf (12)
books.note: The only Quranic sura dedicated entirely to one prophet's story

primaryTitle: Khatib al-Anbiya — The Eloquent of the Prophets
titles: Shu'ayb · Khatib al-Anbiya · father-in-law of Musa in some traditions
school: Prophet sent to the people of Midian; preached against dishonest trade, fraud in weights and measures, and idol worship; considered by some traditions to be the father-in-law of Musa; his people were destroyed when they rejected him

books.title: Quran — Sura al-A'raf (7:85–93)
books.note: The mission of Shu'ayb to the people of Midian

primaryTitle: Sabir — The Patient, Model of Endurance
full: Ayyūb ibn Maws ibn Rā'il ibn 'Isāw
titles: Ayyub · Job · al-Sabir · model of patience and gratitude amid suffering
school: Quranic prophet celebrated above all for his extraordinary patience through severe affliction; after years of suffering he called upon God and was healed and restored; his story is the supreme Quranic model of patient endurance (sabr) and trust in God

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Anbiya (21:83–84)
books.note: Ayyub's supplication and restoration

primaryTitle: Man of the Pledge — Patient and Righteous Prophet
titles: Dhul-Kifl · possibly identified with Ezekiel or Elisha · man of the double portion
school: Mentioned twice in the Quran alongside Ismail and Idris as being among the patient and righteous; identified by some scholars with the biblical Ezekiel; his name (Dhul-Kifl, 'man of the pledge') may refer to a covenant he fulfilled

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Anbiya (21:85–86)
books.note: Brief Quranic mention of Dhul-Kifl among the patient

primaryTitle: Wazir Musa — Minister and Brother of Moses
titles: Harun · Aaron · brother and minister of Musa · Wazir al-Nabi
school: Elder brother of Musa; appointed by God as a prophet and minister (wazir) to assist Musa; known for his eloquence; became the first High Priest of the Israelites; his story is inseparable from that of Musa in the Quranic narrative

books.title: Quran — Sura Ta-Ha (20:29–36)
books.note: Musa's prayer to God to appoint Harun as his helper

primaryTitle: Kalim Allah — He Who Spoke Directly with God
titles: Musa · Kalim Allah · Khatim al-Anbiya al-Isra'iliyyin · Moses · Kalimu'llah
school: One of the five greatest prophets (Ulu al-Azm); most frequently mentioned prophet in the Quran; led the Israelites out of Egypt; received the Torah on Mount Sinai; spoke directly with God without intermediary; the supreme model of divine communication and social liberation

books.title: Quran — Sura Ta-Ha (20)
books.note: A complete retelling of Musa's mission

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Qasas (28)
books.note: The story of Musa from birth through his mission

primaryTitle: Khalifa of God on Earth — Prophet-King and Psalmist
titles: Dawud · David · Khalifat Allah · recipient of the Zabur · slayer of Jalut (Goliath)
school: Prophet-King of Israel; recipient of the Zabur (Psalms); given wisdom and sovereignty; slew the giant Jalut (Goliath) as a young man; his voice in glorification of God caused mountains and birds to join him; father of Sulayman; established Jerusalem as the holy city

books.title: Quran — Sura Saba' (34:10–11)
books.note: God's gifts to Dawud: softening iron and the glorification of mountains

primaryTitle: Malik al-Anbiya — The King of the Prophets
titles: Sulayman · Solomon · builder of Bayt al-Maqdis · master of jinn and wind
school: Son of Dawud; given unparalleled dominion over humans, jinn, animals, and the wind; builder of the Temple in Jerusalem; known for his extraordinary wisdom; the Queen of Sheba (Bilqis) converted under his influence; a whole sura of the Quran is named for the ants (al-Naml) in his story

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Naml (27:15–44)
books.note: The story of Sulayman, the hoopoe, and the Queen of Sheba

primaryTitle: Prophet of Carmel — Reformer Against Idol Worship
full: Ilyās ibn Yasīn ibn Fanhas ibn al-'Ayzār ibn Hārūn
titles: Ilyas · Elijah · taken up to heaven without death according to tradition
school: Prophet of the northern Israelite kingdom; fought against idol worship (Baal) at Mount Carmel; praised in the Quran as among the righteous; his identity with the mysterious figure of Khidr is debated in Islamic tradition; known for miracles and fiery zeal for monotheism

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Saffat (37:123–132)
books.note: The Quranic account of Ilyas among the messengers

primaryTitle: Successor of Ilyas — The Chosen Prophet
titles: Al-Yasa' · Elisha · spiritual successor of Ilyas · chosen above the worlds
school: Prophet of the Israelite kingdom; disciple and spiritual successor of Ilyas; mentioned twice in the Quran as one whom God chose above all others; known in biblical tradition for extraordinary miracles including reviving the dead; carried on Ilyas's mission of monotheism

books.title: Quran — Sura al-An'am (6:86)
books.note: Al-Yasa' listed among the prophets guided by God

primaryTitle: Dhul-Nun — The Man of the Whale
titles: Yunus · Dhul-Nun (Man of the Whale) · Jonah · Sahib al-Hut
school: Prophet sent to Nineveh; left his mission prematurely and was swallowed by a great fish; called upon God from within darkness with the prayer of Dhul-Nun (Tasbih Yunus); his supplication from the belly of the whale is among the most powerful prayers in the Quran; his people repented en masse

books.title: Quran — Sura Yunus (10)
books.note: The sura named for Yunus

books.title: Quran — Sura al-Anbiya (21:87–88)
books.note: Yunus's prayer from within the whale: La ilaha illa Anta

primaryTitle: Guardian of Maryam — Prophet of the Temple
titles: Zakariyya · Zechariah · guardian of Maryam · father of Yahya
school: Temple priest and prophet in Jerusalem; guardian of Maryam (Mary); prayed for a son in old age and was miraculously granted Yahya (John the Baptist); his story in the Quran is told in Sura Maryam; a model of persisting in prayer despite old age and apparent impossibility

books.title: Quran — Sura Maryam (19:2–11)
books.note: The prayer of Zakariyya and the announcement of Yahya

primaryTitle: Sayyid al-Siddiqin — The Chaste, Compassionate Prophet
titles: Yahya · John the Baptist · son of Zakariyya · Sayyid and Hasur (chaste)
school: Son of Zakariyya; given wisdom as a child; described in the Quran as Sayyid (chief/master), Hasur (chaste/restrained), and a prophet; contemporary of Isa; martyred by beheading; a supreme model of purity, compassion, and asceticism in Islamic tradition

books.title: Quran — Sura Maryam (19:12–15)
books.note: God's gifts to Yahya: wisdom, compassion, and purity

primaryTitle: Ruh Allah — Spirit of God, Word of God, Prophet of the Injil
titles: Isa ibn Maryam · al-Masih (the Messiah) · Ruh Allah · Kalimat Allah · Jesus
school: One of the five greatest prophets (Ulu al-Azm); born miraculously of the Virgin Maryam; given the Injil (Gospel); performed miracles by God's leave including healing the blind and raising the dead; in Islam he was neither crucified nor killed but raised by God; will return before the Day of Judgment

books.title: Quran — Sura Maryam (19:16–40)
books.note: The birth of Isa and his first miracle of speaking as an infant

books.title: Quran — Sura Al 'Imran (3:45–55)
books.note: The annunciation and the miracles of Isa

primaryTitle: Jewish Rabbi Who Recognized the Prophet
full: Abd Allah ibn Salam al-Isra'ili al-Ansari
titles: Confirmed in Paradise by the Prophet · Former Jewish Rabbi
school: Leading rabbi of Medina who recognized Muhammad as the prophet foretold in the Torah; one of the Companions whom the Prophet publicly confirmed would be in Paradise; his conversion had a profound impact on Medinan Jewish-Muslim relations

primaryTitle: Mother of the Believers — First Believer
full: Khadīja bint Khuwaylid ibn Asad al-Qurashī
titles: Khadīja al-Kubrā · Ṭāhira (the Pure) · Sayyidat Nisāʾ al-ʿĀlamīn
parents: Khawaylid bin Asad / Fatima bint Za'idah of B.'Amir b. Lu'ayy
spouses: Abu Halah-Malik bin al-Nabash al-Tamimi, Atique bin Aith, Prophet Muhammad(saw)
children: Hind bin Abi Halah , Halah bin Abi Halah , al-Harith bin Abi Halah , Tahir bin Abi Halah , Hindah, al-Qasim bin Muhammad , Zaynab bint Muhammad , Ruqayyah bint Muhammad , Umm Kulthum bint Muhammad , Fatima bint Muhammad , 'Abdullah bin Muhammad

primaryTitle: Siddiq of This Umma — Prophet of the Poor
titles: Siddiq al-Umma · First to greet with al-Salam · Patron of Islamic Socialism
school: Among the first five people to accept Islam; the Prophet declared: 'The earth has not carried nor the sky covered a more truthful man than Abu Dharr'; fiercely opposed the accumulation of wealth; exiled by Uthman to Rabadha for his social critiques; patron saint of Muslim reformers and socialists
parents: Junadah ibn Sakan / Ramla bint al-Waqi'ya al-Ghaffariyya
spouses: Umm Dhur

primaryTitle: Salman al-Khair — Salman the Good; Bridge of Two Worlds
titles: Salman al-Khair · Salman al-Muhammadi
school: Persian Companion of the Prophet — searched for truth across Zoroastrianism and Christianity before reaching Islam; the Prophet declared 'Salman is from us, the Ahl al-Bayt'; patron of Persian Sufism

books.title: Transmitted hadiths and letters
books.url: ,note:"His spiritual journey is the first great conversion narrative in Islamic literature

parents: Yasir ibn 'Amir / Sumayyah bint Khayyat
children: Muhammad bin 'Ammar bin Yasir

primaryTitle: Host of the Prophet — Martyr before Constantinople
full: Khalid ibn Zayd ibn Kulayb al-Ansari al-Najjari
titles: Host of the Prophet · Sahib al-Nabi · Martyr of Constantinople
school: The Prophet's camel stopped at his house upon arrival in Medina — making Abu Ayyub the prophet's first host; fought in dozens of expeditions; died during the Muslim siege of Constantinople aged over 100 and was buried beneath its walls; his tomb near Istanbul is still a pilgrimage site
parents: Zayd ibn Kulayb / Hind bint Sa'eed b. 'Amr
spouses: Umm Ayoub bint Qays bin Sa'd
children: 'Amra bint Abi Ayoub

titles: Khatam al-Anbiya · al-Mustafa · al-Amin · Rahmatan lil-'Alamin
school: Prophetic Foundation — Source of All Sufi Chains

books.title: The Quran (Divine Revelation, not personally authored)
books.note: Not a personal work. Free translations at quran.com

parents: 'Abdullah ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib / Amina bint Wahb b 'Abd Munaf bin Zuhrah

school: Ansari companion known for a profound episode of repentance — when he inadvertently betrayed the Prophet's confidence regarding the Banu Qurayza, he tied himself to a mosque pillar vowing not to untie himself until God forgave him; the Quran references this repentance

primaryTitle: First Martyr Family — Symbol of Early Persecution

titles: Ibn al-Shahidayn (Son of Two Martyrs) · First Builder of Mosque

primaryTitle: Among the First Seven — Early Martyr of Torture

spouses: Mulayka wife Khabbab

primaryTitle: Tribal Chief Who Came to the Prophet

primaryTitle: Companion Whose Eye Was Restored by the Prophet

titles: Sahib Miracle of the Eye · Warrior of Badr
school: Ansari warrior at Badr and Uhud; at Uhud his eye was struck out and hung on his cheek — the Prophet placed it back with his hand and it became the better of his two eyes; this remains one of the best-attested miracles of the Prophet

primaryTitle: First to Introduce Mosque Lamps — Christian Convert
titles: First Mosque Lamp · al-Dari · Christian Companion
school: Christian monk-scholar from Palestine who converted to Islam and became a Companion; introduced the practice of hanging lamps in mosques; famous for narrating the story of al-Jassasa and the Dajjal — one of the few hadiths the Prophet verified in public; pioneered nightly prayer vigils (tahajjud) worship
parents: Aus b. Kharija b. Suwad

primaryTitle: Khatib al-Nabi — Orator of the Prophet

titles: Khatib Rasul Allah (Orator of the Prophet) · Martyr of Yamama
school: The official orator-spokesman of the Prophet; when Quran 49:2 warned against raising one's voice above the Prophet's, he feared himself damned and hid — the Prophet assured him: 'You are not of the hypocrites, you will live with praise and die a martyr'; died heroically at the Battle of Yamama against Musaylima
parents: Thabit bin Qays b. Shammas / Jamila bint 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy
spouses: Umm 'Abdullah bint Hafs of B.Harith b.al-Khazraj, Others

full: Fāṭimah bint Muḥammad ibn ʿAbdillāh al-Hāshimiyya
titles: al-Zahrāʾ (The Radiant) · al-Baṭūl (The Pure) · Umm Abīhā (Mother of Her Father) · Sayyidat Nisāʾ al-ʿĀlamīn (Lady of the Women of the Worlds) · al-Ṣiddīqa

The Prophet declared her the Sayyidat Nisāʾ al-Janna (foremost woman of Paradise) and rose to greet her whenever she entered a room. She died only six months after her father in 632 CE, still mourning his loss, and was the first of his family to follow him in death.

primaryTitle: Umm al-Muʾminīn — Mother of the Believers
full: Hind bint Abi Umayyah ibn al-Mughīra al-Makhzūmiyya
titles: Umm al-Muʾminīn · Zād al-Rākib (for her father's epithet: Provider of the Traveller) · Akhbar al-Umahāt (Most Knowledgeable of the Mothers)
school: Sixth wife of the Prophet Muhammad and the last of the Mothers of the Believers to pass away; she narrated 378 hadiths — the second-highest tally among the Prophet's wives after Aisha. Among the earliest Muslims to migrate to Abyssinia, she endured the forced separation of her husband and infant son during the Hijra. Her counsel at the Treaty of Hudaybiyya, advising the Prophet to lead by example rather than speaking to the companions, averted a potential crisis. The verse of purification (33:33) was revealed in her house.

primaryTitle: Umm al-Muʾminīn — Custodian of the Quran
full: Ḥafṣah bint ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb al-ʿAdawiyya al-Qurashiyya
titles: Umm al-Muʾminīn · Ḥāfiẓat al-Muṣḥaf (Guardian of the Quran Manuscript) · Ṣawwāma Qawwāma (Devoted to Fasting and Prayer)
school: Fourth wife of the Prophet Muhammad and daughter of the second Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab; entrusted by Abu Bakr with the first written compilation of the Quran after the Battle of Yamama, which remained in her home as the master manuscript. Uthman later used her copy as the reference for his standardised Quran. The angel Jibrīl described her to the Prophet as a woman of prayer and fasting who would be his wife in Paradise. She narrated 60 hadiths, with six appearing in both Bukhari and Muslim.

primaryTitle: Umm al-Muʾminīn — Married from Across the Sea
full: Ramlah bint Abi Sufyān Ṣakhr ibn Ḥarb al-Umawiyya al-Qurashiyya
titles: Umm Ḥabībah · Umm al-Muʾminīn · Bint Sayyid al-Quraysh (Daughter of the Quraysh Chief)
school: Among the earliest converts to Islam despite her father Abu Sufyan being the foremost enemy of the Prophet; she migrated to Abyssinia where her husband Ubaydullah ibn Jahsh apostatised. The Negus of Abyssinia conducted her marriage to the Prophet on his behalf as a unique proxy ceremony across the sea. Her steadfast faith in isolation — divorced and abroad — is celebrated as a model of conviction. She narrated 65 hadiths and is ranked third in knowledge among the Mothers of the Believers.

primaryTitle: Dhāt al-Niṭāqayn — She of the Two Belts
full: Asmāʾ bint Abi Bakr ibn Abi Quḥāfa al-Qurashiyya al-Taymiyya
titles: Dhāt al-Niṭāqayn (She of the Two Belts) · Last of the Muhājirūn · Mother of Caliph ʿAbdullāh ibn al-Zubayr
school: Elder half-sister of Aisha and daughter of Abu Bakr; she earned her celebrated title by tearing her belt in two to secure provisions for the Prophet and her father hiding in Cave Thawr before the Hijra — doing so while seven months pregnant. One of the first 17-18 Muslims, she defied Abu Jahl's intimidation at her doorstep and later famously exhorted her son Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr to die with honour rather than surrender to al-Hajjaj. She died in 73 AH aged approximately 100, the last surviving Muhājir. Narrated 58 hadiths.

primaryTitle: Dhāt al-Hijratayn — Woman of Two Migrations
full: Asmāʾ bint ʿUmays ibn Maʿad al-Khathʿamiyya
titles: Dhāt al-Hijratayn (Woman of Two Migrations) · al-Akhawāt al-Muʾmināt (The Believing Sisters — with Maymunah and Umm al-Faḍl)
school: Among the earliest converts to Islam, Asma migrated twice — to Abyssinia with her first husband Jafar ibn Abi Talib, then to Medina. After Jafar's martyrdom at the Battle of Muta (629 CE) she successively married Abu Bakr al-Siddiq and later Ali ibn Abi Talib, making her the sole woman in Islamic history to have been wife to three of the four Rightly Guided Caliphs' closest circle. She was the confidante of Fatimah al-Zahra, washed her body at death per her final wishes, and was present at the most pivotal moments of the Prophetic era.

primaryTitle: Nusaybah bint al-Ḥārith — Authority on the Funeral Bath
titles: Umm ʿAṭiyya · Nusaybah bint al-Ḥārith
school: Anṣārī female companion who accompanied the Prophet on seven military campaigns, nursing the wounded and preparing food. The Prophet personally instructed her in the ritual washing of Zaynab bint Muhammad's body, and her transmission of this procedure became the definitive juristic source across all schools of Islamic law for the funeral bath of women. Al-Ṭabarānī preserved 85 of her traditions; six appear in both Bukhari and Muslim. She later taught in Basra, where Ali ibn Abi Talib visited her.

primaryTitle: Khaṭībat al-Nisāʾ — Orator of the Women
full: Asmāʾ bint Yazīd ibn al-Sakan al-Anṣāriyya al-Awsiyya
titles: Khaṭībat al-Nisāʾ (Orator of the Women) · Safīrat al-Nisāʾ (Spokesperson of the Women)
school: The most eloquent female companion of the Prophet; she famously approached the Prophet as delegate for all Muslim women to raise the question of spiritual equality and reward between men and women — a speech the Prophet praised as surpassing anything he had heard from a woman. She narrated 81 hadiths — the highest count among Anṣārī women. She accompanied the Conquest of Mecca and at the Battle of al-Yarmuk (636 CE) fought with a tent pole, reportedly killing nine Byzantine soldiers. She died in Damascus after a long life of teaching.

primaryTitle: Abū Bakr al-Baṣrī — Imām of the Hadith Scholars of Basra
full: Ayyūb ibn Abi Tamīma Kaysān al-Sakhtiyānī al-Baṣrī
titles: Abū Bakr al-Baṣrī · Sayyid Aḥdāth al-Baṣra (Master of the Youth of Basra — per Hasan al-Basri) · Imām Ahl al-Baṣra
school: One of the foremost authorities among the Tābiʿīn and a towering pillar of Sunni hadith scholarship; Sufyan ibn ʿUyayna said he met 86 Tabi'in but none was like Ayyub. He studied under al-Hasan al-Basri, Nafī Mawlā Ibn ʿUmar, and Muhammad ibn Sirin, and was himself a teacher of Shuʿba ibn al-Ḥajjāj, Sufyan al-Thawri, and Ḥammād ibn Zayd. A resolute defender of Sunnah doctrine against early Muʿtazilite theology, he died in Basra during the great plague of 131 AH.

primaryTitle: Umm Hudhayl — Pioneer of Female Asceticism in Islam
full: Ḥafṣah bint Sīrīn al-Baṣriyya, Mawlāt Anas ibn Mālik
titles: Umm Hudhayl · Iyas ibn Muʿāwiya's verdict: 'I have met no one I prefer over her'
school: 8th-century jurist, ascetic, and Qurʾānic scholar from Basra; she memorised the entire Quran by age twelve and mastered all modes of recitation. Her brother Muhammad ibn Sirin — himself one of the greatest Tabi'un scholars — deferred to her on questions of Qurʾānic recitation. The judge Iyas ibn Muʿāwiya, asked to compare her to Hasan al-Basri and her own brother Muhammad, answered 'I do not prefer anyone over her.' Called a 'pioneer of female asceticism in Islam' by later scholars. Narrated 17 hadiths primarily from Anas ibn Malik and Umm Atiyya.

primaryTitle: Baḥr al-ʿIlm — The Ocean of Knowledge
full: ʿAmrah bint ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn Saʿd ibn Zurāra al-Anṣāriyya al-Najjāriyya al-Madaniyya
titles: Baḥr al-ʿIlm (Ocean of Knowledge — per Ibn Shihāb al-Zuhrī) · Kātibat ʿĀʾisha (Secretary of Aisha)
school: The foremost transmitter of Aisha's prophetic traditions; raised in Aisha's household after her father's death, she absorbed the complete corpus of Aisha's hadith knowledge. Al-Zuhrī called her 'an inexhaustible ocean' after his first visit; Caliph Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz ordered the official recording of her narrations alone. Her juridical rulings overrode male judges in Medina — as in the famous case of the Christian thief whose hand she prevented from being cut — accepted without challenge by the sitting judge. Imam Sufyan ibn ʿUyayna ranked her alongside al-Qasim ibn Muhammad and Urwah ibn al-Zubayr as the three most authoritative transmitters of Aisha's hadiths.

primaryTitle: Step-Daughter of the Prophet — Born in Abyssinia
full: Zaynab bint ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbd al-Asad al-Makhzūmiyya (born Barra; renamed Zaynab by the Prophet)
titles: Zaynab (renamed by the Prophet from Barra) · Rabībat Rasūlillāh (Step-Daughter of the Messenger)
school: Born in Abyssinia during the first Muslim migration, step-daughter of the Prophet through his marriage to her mother Umm Salamah. The Prophet renamed her Zaynab and raised her in his household with great affection — reportedly splashing blessed water on her face in play, a gesture said to have preserved her youthful appearance into old age. She transmitted hadith from Umm Salamah, Aisha, Zaynab bint Jahsh, and Umm Habibah, forming a rare first-hand bridge to the inner household of the Prophet. Eight of her traditions are preserved by al-Ṭabarānī.

books.title: al-Ṭabarānī — al-Muʿjam al-Kabīr
books.note: Preserves eight of her traditions with chains of transmission

books.note: Primary biographical source on her birth, naming, and transmissions

primaryTitle: Niece of Aisha bint Abi Bakr
full: ʿĀʾisha bint Ṭalḥa ibn ʿUbaydillāh al-Taymiyya al-Qurashiyya
titles: Bint Ṭalḥa · Bint al-Ṣaḥābi al-Shahīd (Daughter of the Martyred Companion)
school: Daughter of Talha ibn Ubaydillah — one of the Ten Companions Promised Paradise — and niece of Aisha bint Abi Bakr through her mother Umm Kulthum bint Abi Bakr. She grew up in close contact with her famous aunt and became a significant transmitter of Aisha's hadith corpus. Her name appears as a key node in the isnad network linking the household of the first Caliph Abu Bakr to the second and third hadith generations. She is cited as a narrator by major successors including Muawiya ibn Ishaq and Ibn Abi Najih.

books.title: Ibn Saʿd — al-Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā (Vol. 8)
books.note: Biographical entry on the daughters of the companions

books.note: Rijal reference confirming her as a reliable female narrator

books.note: Assessment of her place in the hadith transmission chain

source: Ibn Saʿd — Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā · al-Mizzī — Tahdhīb al-Kamāl · Ibn Ḥajar — Tahdhīb al-Tahdhīb

primaryTitle: Narrator from the Mothers of the Believers
full: Ṣafiyya bint Shayba ibn ʿUthmān ibn Abi Ṭalḥa al-ʿAbdariyya al-Qurashiyya
titles: Ṣafiyya bint Shayba · min Ahl Makkah (Meccan Companion)
school: Female companion from the Custodians of the Kaaba (Banu Shayba) who narrated traditions directly from several of the Prophet's wives, forming a critical link in the hadith transmission network radiating from the Mothers of the Believers. Narrated from Aisha, Umm Salamah, Umm Habibah and Maymunah, as well as from the companion Asma bint Abi Bakr. Her son Abd al-Hamid ibn Jubayr and grandson Umayyah ibn Safwan were themselves transmitters who carried her traditions to the second generation.

books.title: Ibn Saʿd — al-Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā (Vol. 8)
books.note: Primary biographical source for her entries among the female companions

books.note: Rijal assessment confirming her reliability as a narrator

primaryTitle: One of the Seven Jurists of Medina
full: Abu Salamah ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn ʿAwf al-Qurashi al-Zuhrī al-Madanī
titles: Aḥad al-Fuqahāʾ al-Sabʿa (One of the Seven Jurists of Medina) · Abū Salamah
school: Son of Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf — one of the Ten Companions Promised Paradise — and a leading Medinan jurist counted among the Seven Jurists of Medina (al-Fuqahāʾ al-Sabʿa) whose legal opinions formed the foundation of Medinan and Maliki jurisprudence. He transmitted extensively from Aisha, Umm Salamah, Abu Hurayrah, and other companions, and was himself a teacher of Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri, Yahya ibn Said al-Ansari, and Muhammad ibn Ibrahim al-Taymi.

books.title: al-Dhahabī — Siyar Aʿlām al-Nubalāʾ (Vol. 4)
books.note: Detailed entry on his juristic career and standing among the Seven Jurists

primaryTitle: Great-Niece of Aisha — Wife of Hisham ibn Urwah
full: Fāṭima bint al-Munthir ibn al-Zubayr ibn al-ʿAwwām al-Qurashiyya al-Asadiyya
titles: Zawjat Hishām ibn ʿUrwa (Wife of Hisham ibn Urwah)
school: Granddaughter of Asma bint Abi Bakr and great-niece of Aisha bint Abi Bakr; married to the prominent hadith scholar Hisham ibn Urwah ibn al-Zubayr (her cousin). She transmitted traditions from her grandmother Asma bint Abi Bakr, forming a key isnad link connecting the household of Abu Bakr to the third hadith generation through the prominent Zubayr family of Medina. Her narrations appear in the major hadith collections transmitted by her husband Hisham.

books.title: al-Mizzī — Tahdhīb al-Kamāl fī Asmāʾ al-Rijāl
books.note: Rijal entry identifying her place in the isnad chains through Hisham ibn Urwah

primaryTitle: Anṣārī Companion — First Khulʿ Case in Islam
full: Ḥabība bint Sahl ibn Thaʿlaba al-Anṣāriyya al-Khazrajiyya al-Madaniyya
titles: Ḥabībat Rasūlillāh (from her proximity to the Prophet's household) · Ṣāḥibat Awwal Khulʿ (Subject of the First Khulʿ Case)
school: Anṣārī female companion of the Prophet from Medina whose case with Thabit ibn Qays became one of the earliest and most cited precedents in Islamic jurisprudence for the khulʿ — the wife-initiated dissolution of marriage by returning the mahr. She came to the Prophet at dawn to request separation, a case recorded in canonical hadith collections. Her narrative contributed directly to the formation of Islamic family law on women's right to seek divorce.

books.title: al-Bukhāri — Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhāri (Kitab al-Talaq)
books.note: Contains the canonical account of her khulʿ case

primaryTitle: She Who Memorised Sūrah Qāf from the Prophet
full: Umm Hishām bint Ḥāritha ibn al-Nuʿmān al-Anṣāriyya al-Khazrajiyya al-Madaniyya
titles: Bāyiʿat al-Shajara (She Who Pledged at the Tree) · Ḥāfizat Sūrat Qāf
school: Anṣārī female companion who participated in Bayʿat al-Riḍwān — the Pledge of the Tree at Hudaybiyya praised by a Qurʾānic verse (48:18). She memorised Sūrah Qāf directly from the Prophet Muhammad's repeated recitation of it during his Friday khutba sermons; her account of this is preserved in Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim and became the canonical transmission of that sūrah's oral tradition. A close neighbour of the Prophet, she and Amrah bint Abd al-Rahman both transmitted from her half-sister.

books.title: Muslim — Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim (Kitab al-Jumua)
books.note: Contains her famous account of memorising Sūrat Qāf from the Prophet's Friday sermons

primaryTitle: Anṣārī Companion — Witness to Prophetic Traditions
titles: Umm Mubashshir · min Anṣār al-Nabī (Of the Supporters of the Prophet)
school: Anṣārī female companion from Medina who transmitted traditions directly from the Prophet Muhammad, preserved in the major hadith collections. She is recorded in the biographical dictionaries as a reliable transmitter among the women of the Ansar, and her narrations — including an account of a conversation with the Prophet at Khaybar about the Muslim dead — are cited in the canonical collections. Her son Mubashshir ibn Zayd was also a transmitter of hadith.

books.title: Ibn Saʿd — al-Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā (Vol. 8)

primaryTitle: Wife of ʿAbdullāh ibn ʿUmar — Key Narrator of the Umar Household
full: Ṣafiyya bint Abi ʿUbayd ibn Masʿūd al-Thaqafiyya al-Madaniyya
titles: Zawjat Ibn ʿUmar (Wife of Ibn Umar) · Bint Abi ʿUbayd al-Thaqafī
school: Wife of the famous companion Abdullah ibn Umar ibn al-Khattab; through her marriage she was positioned at the nexus of hadith transmission connecting the household of Umar ibn al-Khattab to the second generation. She transmitted from Abdullah ibn Umar, Aisha bint Abi Bakr, and Hafsa bint Umar (her sister-in-law), and her narrations appear in the major collections. Her family's lineage from the Thaqif tribe and her husband's eminent status as one of the most prolific hadith narrators gave her traditions particular weight.

books.title: Ibn Saʿd — al-Ṭabaqāt al-Kubrā (Vol. 8)
books.note: Biographical details on the wives and daughters in the Umar ibn al-Khattab household chain

primaryTitle: The Child Who Made Hajj with the Prophet
full: al-Sāʾib ibn Yazīd ibn Saʿīd ibn Thumāma al-Kindī al-Azdī al-Madanī
titles: al-Sāʾib · Min Ṣighār al-Ṣaḥāba (Among the Young Companions)
school: A child companion who performed the Farewell Pilgrimage with the Prophet Muhammad at the age of seven, an event he recorded in the canonical hadith of al-Bukhari (no. 1858) — 'I was taken to perform Hajj with the Messenger of Allah when I was seven years old.' As a child companion (min ṣighār al-ṣaḥāba) he lived long enough to transmit traditions to the Tabi'un and became a treasured living link to the Prophetic era; he also served as a market overseer (ṣāḥib al-sūq) in Medina under Umar ibn al-Khattab.

books.title: al-Bukhāri — Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhāri (Kitab al-Hajj, Hadith 1858)
books.note: The canonical hadith in which he recounts performing Hajj with the Prophet at age seven

books.note: Biography among the younger companions and his role in Medinan administration

primaryTitle: Umm al-Mu'minin — Mother of the Believers
full: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid ibn Asad al-Qurashiyya al-Asadiyya
classif: Companion / First Muslim / Mother of the Believers
titles: Umm al-Mu'minin (Mother of the Believers) · al-Tahira (the Pure) · Sayyidat Nisa al-Alamin (Lady of the Women of the Worlds) · Khadijah al-Kubra (the Great)
school: First wife of Prophet Muhammad and the very first person to embrace Islam; wealthy and respected Qurayshi merchant who proposed marriage to the Prophet in 595 CE. Her wealth, unwavering support and emotional strength were the bedrock of the early Muslim community. When the Prophet received the first revelation and was gripped with fear, it was Khadijah who reassured him, wrapped him in a cloak, and affirmed his prophethood. She financed the mission, ransomed persecuted Muslim slaves, and supported the community through the three-year economic boycott in the Shi'b Abi Talib. She bore him six children, including Fatimah al-Zahra. She died in 619 CE, the year the Prophet called 'Year of Sorrow'. Angel Jibril conveyed Allah's salaam to her. She is counted among the four greatest women of all time alongside Maryam, Asiya and Fatimah.

books.title: Khadijah, Daughter of Khuwaylid — Al-Islam.org
books.note: Detailed Arabic and English biography

full: Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim ibn Abd Manaf al-Qurayshi al-Hashimi
dod_s: March 625 CE / 3 AH — Battle of Uhud
titles: Asad Allah (Lion of Allah) · Asad Rasul Allah (Lion of the Messenger of Allah) · Sayyid al-Shuhada (Chief/Master of the Martyrs) · Abu Umara
school: Paternal uncle and foster brother of Prophet Muhammad; one of the most formidable warriors in early Islam. Converted to Islam in 616 CE after striking Abu Jahl who had insulted the Prophet, entering Dar al-Arqam immediately after. His conversion was a turning point that gave the early Muslim community courage and strength. He commanded the first Muslim military expedition. At the Battle of Badr (624 CE), he fought with two swords and was credited with decisive individual combat. At Uhud (625 CE), he was treacherously slain by Wahshi ibn Harb, an Abyssinian javelin-thrower hired by Hind bint Utba for revenge. His martyrdom devastated the Prophet, who wept and is narrated to have said that Jibril referred to him as 'the Lion of the Prophet' in the seven heavens. The Prophet called him 'Sayyid al-Shuhada'. His brave death is referenced in Surah al-Nahl (Q16:125-128).

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

quranRef: Q16:125-128 (revealed in response to his martyrdom)

primaryTitle: Zayd al-Hubb — The Beloved of the Prophet
full: Zayd ibn Harithah ibn Sharahil al-Kalbi al-Quda'i
dod_s: 629 CE / 8 AH — Battle of Mu'tah, Jordan
classif: Companion / Commander / Freed Slave / Adopted Son of the Prophet
titles: Zayd al-Hubb (the Beloved) · Abu Usama · Formerly known as Zayd ibn Muhammad (before the verse abrogating adoption)
school: Fourth person to accept Islam; the only companion whose name is explicitly mentioned in the Quran (Surah al-Ahzab 33:37). Originally from the Kalb tribe of Yemen, he was captured as a child and sold into slavery, purchased for Khadijah bint Khuwaylid who gave him as a gift to the Prophet. When his father came to ransom him, Zayd chose to remain with the Prophet, who then publicly adopted him at the Kaaba. He converted to Islam at the very beginning of the mission. The Prophet never sent any army without appointing Zayd as commander (Aisha's testimony). He participated in the Battle of Badr and commanded numerous expeditions. He was martyred at the Battle of Mu'tah (629 CE) commanding a force of 3,000 Muslims against a 200,000-strong Byzantine army. The Prophet wept at his grave, calling him 'the yearning of the lover for the beloved'.
quranRef: Q33:37 — Only companion mentioned by name in the Quran

full: Jafar ibn Abi Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim al-Qurayshi al-Hashimi
dod_s: September 629 CE / 8 AH — Battle of Mu'tah, Jordan
titles: Jafar al-Tayyar (the Flyer) · Abu al-Masakin (Father of the Poor) · Dhu al-Janahayn (He of Two Wings) · Dhu al-Hijratayn (He of Two Migrations)
school: Cousin of Prophet Muhammad and elder brother of Ali ibn Abi Talib; among the earliest converts to Islam. Led the first migration (hijra) to Abyssinia in 616 CE, where he lived for twelve years. Served as the eloquent spokesman for the Muslim community before the Negus (King of Abyssinia), delivering one of the most celebrated early defenses of Islam, reciting the opening of Surah Maryam and moving the Christian king to tears. The Prophet rejoiced at his return from Abyssinia in 628 CE as much as at the conquest of Khaybar. Known for extraordinary generosity to the poor. Martyred at the Battle of Mu'tah (629 CE) while commanding the army after Zayd ibn Harithah fell; he fought until both arms were severed, holding the banner against his chest. The Prophet reported that Jibril said Allah had given Jafar two wings to fly in Paradise in place of his severed arms.

primaryTitle: Safeer al-Islam — First Ambassador of Islam
full: Musab ibn Umayr ibn Hashim ibn Abd al-Dar al-Qurashi al-Abdari
titles: Safeer al-Islam (Ambassador of Islam) · al-Muqri (the Reciter/Teacher) · Abu Abd Allah
school: Handsome, wealthy youth of the Quraysh who embraced Islam in 614 CE in the house of al-Arqam and endured severe family persecution for his faith. After emigrating to Abyssinia, he was chosen by the Prophet as Islam's first diplomatic envoy — sent to Yathrib (Madinah) after the First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE) to teach Quran, prepare the city for the Hijra, and unite the Aws and Khazraj tribes. His mission was transformative: within a year, senior tribal chiefs including Sa'd ibn Muadh and Usayd ibn Hudayr embraced Islam, leading to mass conversions. He also led the first Friday Jumu'ah prayer ever performed in Madinah. He carried the Muslim flag at the Battle of Badr and at Uhud, where he was martyred — dying so poor he was shrouded in a single patched cloth barely long enough to cover him fully.
quranRef: Q33:23 — 'Among the believers are men true to their pledge'

primaryTitle: Sayyid al-Aws — Chief of the Aws Tribe
full: Sa'd ibn Muadh ibn al-Numan ibn Imru al-Qays al-Ansari al-Awsi
dod_s: 627 CE / 5 AH — from wounds at Battle of the Trench
classif: Companion / Tribal Chief / Ansar Leader / Martyr
titles: Sayyid al-Aws (Chief of the Aws tribe) · Siddiq al-Ansar (Truthful Man of the Ansar) · Abu Amr
school: Chief of the Banu Abd al-Ashhal clan of the Aws tribe in Madinah; one of the most pivotal Ansar companions. Converted to Islam in 622 CE (1 AH) through Musab ibn Umayr, whereupon his entire subtribe of Banu Abd al-Ashhal — the first household in Madinah — converted en masse, breaking their idols. His conversion and leadership were critical to the social foundation of the Islamic state in Madinah. He participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud, and the Trench. At Badr he was the standard-bearer of the Aws. At the Battle of the Trench (627 CE), he was struck by an arrow that severed a main vein; he lived long enough to deliver his famous verdict against the treacherous Banu Qurayza, which the Prophet declared was 'the judgment of Allah'. He died of his wounds at age 37. The Prophet stood barefoot at his funeral and declared 'The Throne of the Most Merciful shook at the death of Sa'd ibn Muadh.' Narrated that 70,000 angels attended his funeral prayer.

primaryTitle: Shahid al-Yamamah — Martyr of the Battle of Yamamah
full: Zayd ibn al-Khattab ibn Nufayl al-Adawi al-Qurashi
dod_s: December 632 CE / 12 AH — Battle of Yamamah
titles: Hawk of Yamamah · Abu Abd al-Rahman · Elder Brother of Umar al-Faruq
school: Elder half-brother of Umar ibn al-Khattab; converted to Islam before Umar, preceding him in this distinction. Among the earliest Muhajirun who emigrated to Madinah; the Prophet established a bond of brotherhood between him and Ma'n ibn Adi. Participated in the Battle of Badr, Uhud, Khandaq and all campaigns with the Prophet, refusing armour at Uhud so as to be equal with his brother in seeking martyrdom. After the Prophet's death (632 CE), he served as deputy commander under Khalid ibn al-Walid in the Ridda Wars. At the Battle of Yamamah against the false prophet Musaylimah, Zayd served as standard-bearer. When Muslims wavered, he rallied them with a stirring speech, fought until slain, still holding the banner. Umar mourned deeply, saying 'My brother surpassed me in two virtues: he embraced Islam before me and was martyred before me.' His martyrdom alongside many Quran memorisers prompted Umar to urge Abu Bakr to commission the first collection of the Quran.

primaryTitle: One of the Four Quran Teachers of the Prophet
full: Salim ibn Ma'qil, Mawla Abi Hudhayfah ibn Utba al-Qurashi
titles: Salim Mawla Abi Hudhayfah · Abu Abd Allah
school: Persian-born freed slave adopted by Abu Hudhayfah ibn Utba of the Quraysh. Among the earliest Muslim converts; one of four primary Quran teachers personally recommended by the Prophet. The Prophet said: 'Learn the Quran from four persons: Abdullah ibn Masud, Salim Mawla Abi Hudhayfah, Ubayy ibn Kab and Muadh ibn Jabal.' Aisha heard his beautiful Quran recitation one night; the Prophet came to listen and praised Allah for placing someone like Salim in the Ummah. Participated in Badr and all major battles. At the Battle of Yamamah (632 CE) he held the Muslim banner until his right hand was severed, then held it with his left, reciting Quran until martyrdom. Umar, dying years later, said 'If Salim were alive, I would have appointed him my successor.' His death was one of the chief motivations for compiling the Quran into a single written volume.

primaryTitle: Early Emigrant and Martyr of Yamamah
full: Abu Hudhayfah Qays ibn Utba ibn Rabi'a ibn Abd Shams al-Qurashi
titles: Abu Hudhayfah · Father of Salim (by adoption)
school: Noble Qurayshi companion from Banu Abd Shams; son of the leading Quraysh chieftain Utba ibn Rabi'a. Embraced Islam before the Prophet entered Dar al-Arqam — among the very earliest Muslims. Participated in both migrations to Abyssinia where his son Muhammad was born. Participated in the Battle of Badr where he faced the tragic position of his own father fighting against the Muslims. Fought at Uhud, Khandaq and all major engagements. He adopted Salim, the freed Persian slave who became one of the great Quran teachers. At the Battle of Yamamah (632 CE), he and Salim embraced, made a vow of martyrdom together, shouted 'O People of the Quran!' to rally the Muslims, and both were found slain side by side — one's head at the feet of the other.

primaryTitle: Founder of the City of Basra
full: Utbah ibn Ghazwan ibn Jabir ibn Wahb al-Mazini, Confederate of Banu Nawfal
dod_s: 639 CE / 17 AH — died from injuries near Madinah
titles: Seventh Muslim · Founder of Basra · First Governor of Basra · Abu Ghuzayyah
school: Considered the seventh person to embrace Islam. Participated in both migrations to Abyssinia. Tall and impressive, known for exceptional skill with spear and arrow; participated in Badr, Uhud, Khandaq, Yamamah and many campaigns. Under Caliph Umar, he commanded 2,000 men against the Persian garrison at Ubullah (Iraq) in 635 CE, opening lower Mesopotamia to Islam. He founded the garrison city of Basra — one of the great cities of the Islamic world — planning it strategically between the Arabian Desert and the Persian Gulf, building its first mosque. Concerned that Muslims were softening to worldly comforts, he delivered a famous sermon on zuhd (detachment): recalling early Islam when he and Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas shared a single cloak. He asked Umar to relieve him of governorship; Umar refused, and on his return journey to Basra he fell from his camel and died.

primaryTitle: Trusted Special Envoy and Swordsman of the Prophet
full: Muhammad ibn Maslama ibn Khalid al-Ansari al-Awsi al-Harithiyyi
titles: Abu Abd Allah · Trusted Special Envoy of the Prophet · Swordsman of the Ansar
school: Ansari companion from the Aws tribe, Banu Haritha clan; an early Medinan convert. Participated in the Battle of Badr, Uhud and Khandaq. Renowned as the Prophet's most trusted special envoy and special operations commander: assigned delicate, sensitive missions including leadership of the mission against Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf who had incited war against the Muslims with his poetry, as well as numerous other expeditions. He held himself completely apart from the first Fitna (civil war) after Uthman's murder, refusing absolutely to fight any Muslim regardless of faction, maintaining strict neutrality — breaking his sword to symbolise this — a principled stance that distinguished him from virtually all other companions. He died at approximately age 70 in Madinah during the caliphate of Muawiya, one of the last of the Badr companions to die.

primaryTitle: Al-Fayyadh — The Generous; Shield of the Prophet at Uhud
full: Talha ibn Ubaydullah ibn Uthman al-Taymi al-Qurashi
dod_s: 656 CE / 36 AH — Battle of the Camel, Basra
classif: Companion / One of Ten Promised Paradise / Martyr
titles: Al-Fayyadh (The Generous) · Al-Khayr (The Good) · From the Asharah Mubasharah
school: One of the Asharah Mubasharah — the Ten Companions explicitly promised Paradise by the Prophet ﷺ. Among the earliest Muslims, converting before the Prophet entered Dar al-Arqam; one of the eight who first accepted Islam. At the Battle of Uhud (625 CE), he shielded the Prophet with his own body when archers broke rank; he sustained 24 wounds and lost the use of two fingers, but kept the Prophet from falling. The Prophet later said of this day: 'Whoever wishes to see a martyr walking the earth, let him look at Talha ibn Ubaydullah.' Known throughout the Muslim community as 'Al-Fayyadh' (the Bountiful) for his extraordinary generosity — he once distributed 700,000 dirhams in a single day to those in need. He fought at Badr, Uhud, and all major campaigns. He was martyred at the Battle of the Camel (36 AH / 656 CE) in Basra, caught in the civil conflict over the caliphate. He is buried in Basra.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Safeer al-Islam — First Ambassador of Islam
full: Mus'ab ibn Umayr ibn Hashim ibn Abd al-Dar al-Qurashi
titles: Safeer (Envoy of the Prophet)
school: The first diplomat in Islamic history and one of the most remarkable transformation stories among the companions. Born to one of the wealthiest Quraysh families, he was famous for his beauty, fine clothes, and luxury — yet he abandoned everything for Islam, converting secretly around 614 CE in the house of al-Arqam. When his family discovered his conversion, they imprisoned and tortured him. He emigrated to Abyssinia. After the First Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE), the Prophet appointed him as Islam's first official envoy to Madinah — charged with teaching Quran, uniting the fractious Aws and Khazraj tribes, and preparing the city for the Prophet's arrival. Within a year, his mission had transformed Madinah: senior tribal chiefs including Sa'd ibn Muadh embraced Islam, and mass conversions followed. He led the first Friday Jumu'ah prayer in Madinah. He carried the Muslim standard at Badr and Uhud, where he was martyred while defending the Prophet. He died so poor that there was only one small cloth to cover him — when placed over his head, his feet showed; when placed over his feet, his head showed. The Prophet wept. He is buried on the battlefield of Uhud.

primaryTitle: Katib al-Wahy — Scribe of the Revelation
titles: Katib al-Wahy (Scribe of Revelation)
school: One of the Kuttab al-Wahy — the personal scribes of the Quranic revelation appointed by Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Participated in the battles of Badr, Uhud, Khaybar. Died as a martyr (629 CE) at Syria, Mau'ta. Known as a poet among the companions.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: First Wife after Khadijah; Mother of the Believers
full: Sawda bint Zama'a ibn Qays al-Amiriyya
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)
school: Second wife of the Prophet ﷺ, married in Makkah shortly after the death of Khadijah (619 CE), becoming the first woman to carry the title Umm al-Muminin after Khadijah. Her first husband, al-Sakran ibn Amr, had emigrated with her to Abyssinia, where he died. She was a large, jovial woman of great generosity, known for her sharp humor — the Prophet would sometimes laugh until he leaned back. She participated in the farewell pilgrimage. When she grew older, she gave her designated night with the Prophet to Aisha out of love for the Prophet and wishing her to receive what she herself could not provide. The Prophet accepted. Aisha later said: 'I never envied anyone as much as I envied Sawda for her strong character.' She survived the Prophet and died during the caliphate of Muawiyah, approximately 54 AH.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Umm al-Masakin — Mother of the Poor
full: Zaynab bint Khuzayma ibn al-Harith al-Hilaliyya
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)
school: Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers, known throughout the Muslim community as 'Umm al-Masakin' (Mother of the Poor) for her extraordinary generosity to the destitute — a title she carried from before her marriage to the Prophet. She had previously been widowed from Abd Allah ibn Jahsh, who was martyred at Uhud. Her marriage to the Prophet was brief; she died approximately two or three months after the marriage, making her one of only two wives of the Prophet who died during his lifetime (the other being Khadijah). She was buried in Madinah. Her short life is a testament to the Prophet's care for widows of the martyred companions.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Zaynab bint Jahsh — Married by Divine Command
full: Zaynab bint Jahsh ibn Riyab al-Asadiyya
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)
school: Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers. Her marriage to the Prophet was explicitly commanded by Quranic revelation — she had previously been married to Zayd ibn Harithah, the Prophet's adopted son, and after their divorce the Prophet was commanded to marry her to establish that adoption does not carry the same legal weight as biological kinship (Q33:37 — the only verse that mentions a companion by name). She would often boast to the other wives: 'Your families gave you in marriage, but Allah gave me in marriage from above seven heavens.' She was the cousin of the Prophet, from the Banu Asad clan. Known for her deep piety, she would spend entire nights in prayer. Umar once commented that she would spend the whole of Ramadan in prayer and fast. She was extraordinarily generous — when Umar sent her the allowance from the treasury, she distributed it entirely to the poor before the messenger had left. She died in 641 CE / 20 AH and was the first of the Prophet's wives to die after him. The Prophet said of her: 'The quickest of you to join me will be the one with the longest arms' — interpreted as generosity.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

quranRef: Q33:37 — the only Quranic verse mentioning a companion (Zayd) by name

primaryTitle: Juwayriya bint al-Harith — Her Marriage Freed a Hundred Families
full: Juwayriya bint al-Harith ibn Abi Dirar al-Mustaliqqiyya
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)
school: Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers. Daughter of al-Harith ibn Abi Dirar, chief of the Banu al-Mustaliq tribe. She was taken captive when the Muslims defeated the Banu al-Mustaliq in 627 CE / 5 AH and was allocated to Thabit ibn Qays as a captive. She negotiated a contract of manumission with Thabit and came to the Prophet to ask for help fulfilling it. He offered to pay her freedom price and marry her. When this news spread among the Sahabah, they said: 'These are now the in-laws of the Prophet' and freed every captive of the Banu al-Mustaliq in their possession — approximately one hundred families in a single day. Aisha remarked: 'I know of no woman who was a greater blessing to her people than Juwayriya.' She was known for constant remembrance of Allah (dhikr) and long prayers. The Prophet once found her still in her prayer spot at midday; he taught her a short formula of dhikr that equaled the reward of the entire morning in speech. She survived the Prophet and died c. 670 CE.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Umm Habibah — Daughter of Abu Sufyan; Married by the Negus
full: Ramla bint Abi Sufyan ibn Harb al-Umawiyya (Umm Habibah)
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)
school: Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers. Born Ramla, daughter of Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, the leader of Quraysh and Islam's greatest opponent at the time. She embraced Islam early and emigrated to Abyssinia with her first husband, Ubaydullah ibn Jahsh. In Abyssinia, her husband converted to Christianity and died there, leaving her widowed in a foreign land. The Prophet sent a proposal of marriage through the Christian king Negus, who accepted on behalf of the Prophet and paid her the marriage gift (mahr) — the Negus himself performed the marriage ceremony in Abyssinia (628 CE), making this the only marriage in Islamic history conducted by proxy, by a non-Muslim king. She returned to Madinah after the Treaty of Hudaybiyya. When her father Abu Sufyan later came to Madinah to renew the treaty, she refused to let him sit on the Prophet's mat, saying: 'It is the Prophet's mat and you are an unclean polytheist.' Her commitment to Islam over family loyalty became legendary. After the conquest of Makkah (630 CE), her father Abu Sufyan finally accepted Islam. She died c. 666 CE.

books.title: Wikipedia — Umm Habibah bint Abi Sufyan
Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Safiyya bint Huyayy — From the Tribe of Aaron and Moses
full: Safiyya bint Huyayy ibn Akhtab al-Nadriyya
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)
school: Wife of the Prophet ﷺ and Mother of the Believers. Daughter of Huyayy ibn Akhtab, chief of the Banu Nadir Jewish tribe of Khaybar. After the Muslim victory at Khaybar (628 CE), she came under Muslim protection as a captive. The Prophet gave her the choice of returning to her people or accepting Islam and marriage — she chose Islam. She was known for her intelligence, dignity, and deep devotion. Once, when another wife taunted her with 'You are the daughter of a Jew,' she went to the Prophet in tears; he comforted her: 'You could have answered: My father is Aaron, my uncle is Moses, and my husband is Muhammad — so how are you better than me?' She narrated hadith and was known for her generosity. Her conversion represents the universality of Islam's message — from the tribe of the greatest prophets to being a Mother of the Believers. She died approximately 670 CE.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Last Wife of the Prophet; Maymuna bint al-Harith
full: Maymuna bint al-Harith ibn Hazn al-Hilaliyya
titles: Umm al-Muminin (Mother of the Believers)
school: The last woman the Prophet ﷺ married, in 629 CE / 7 AH during the Umrat al-Qada (compensatory Umrah). According to Maymuna herself, she offered herself to the Prophet in marriage while he was mounted on his camel — the Quran acknowledges this in Q33:50 (a believing woman who gives herself to the Prophet). She was the sister-in-law of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib (Abbas married her sister Umm al-Fadl). Known for her piety and generosity; Aisha remarked that 'she was among the most God-fearing of us and the most attentive to the ties of kinship.' She spent her final years in Saraf near Makkah, at the same location where her marriage contract was concluded, at her own request — she wished to be buried where her marriage was completed. She died approximately 674 CE and is buried in Saraf.

primaryTitle: Daughter of the Prophet; Twice Married to Uthman ibn Affan
full: Umm Kulthum bint Muhammad ibn Abd Allah al-Hashimiyya
school: Daughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and Khadijah bint Khuwaylid. She was betrothed before the Prophet's revelation to Utayba ibn Abi Lahab. When Q111 (Surah al-Masad) was revealed condemning Abu Lahab and his family, Abu Lahab ordered his son to divorce her, and he did so, spitting at her in insult. She emigrated to Madinah after the Hijra. After her sister Ruqayya died (624 CE), the Prophet married her to Uthman ibn Affan — the same companion who had married and lost her sister. This is why Uthman is called 'Dhu al-Nurayn' (Possessor of Two Lights) — he married two daughters of the Prophet. She died 631 CE / 9 AH, in the ninth year of the Hijra, and the Prophet himself helped lower her into her grave.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

full: Zaynab bint Muhammad ibn Abd Allah al-Hashimiyya
school: Eldest daughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and Khadijah bint Khuwaylid. She was married to Abu al-As ibn al-Rabi, her maternal cousin, before the Prophet's revelation. When the Prophet declared his mission and she embraced Islam, her husband Abu al-As initially remained on the Quraysh side. At the Battle of Badr, Abu al-As was captured by the Muslims; Zaynab sent her own mother's necklace as ransom — when the Prophet saw it, his eyes filled with tears and he asked the companions if they would free Abu al-As without ransom as a favour to Zaynab. They agreed. Abu al-As kept his word, sending Zaynab to Madinah; she emigrated alone under Quraysh harassment, suffering a miscarriage during her journey due to the attack of Habbar ibn al-Aswad. The Prophet wept at her arrival and prayed against those who had attacked her. Abu al-As later accepted Islam (627 CE) and was reunited with Zaynab. She died 629 CE / 8 AH.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Aqila Banu Hashim — The Wise Woman of Banu Hashim; Voice of Karbala
full: Zaynab bint Ali ibn Abi Talib al-Hashimiyya
school: Daughter of Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah al-Zahra, and granddaughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She is among the most venerated women in Islamic history for her role at and after the Battle of Karbala (680 CE / 61 AH). She witnessed the massacre of her brother Husayn and 72 companions, the deaths of her sons and nephews, and was taken as a prisoner by Yazid ibn Muawiyah to Damascus. In Yazid's court, she delivered one of the most powerful speeches in Arabic oratory — denouncing the tyranny of Yazid to his face, before his entire court, despite being in captivity. She refused humiliation, declaring: 'Do you think you have closed the gates of heaven on us? You have not. You have killed the bodies of noble men, but you cannot kill what they stood for.' Her speech turned public opinion and preserved the narrative of Karbala for all time. She is called 'Aqila Banu Hashim' (the Wise Woman of the House of the Prophet). She returned to Madinah and later moved to Egypt or Syria, where she died c. 682 CE.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Granddaughter of the Prophet; Present at Karbala
full: Umm Kulthum bint Ali ibn Abi Talib al-Hashimiyya
school: Daughter of Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah al-Zahra, and granddaughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She was present at the Battle of Karbala (680 CE / 61 AH) alongside her sister Zaynab bint Ali and witnessed the martyrdom of their brother Husayn and the companions. After Karbala she was taken as a prisoner alongside her sister to Kufa and then Damascus. She narrated hadith and is cited in classical sources. She is buried in Damascus according to one tradition, though other accounts differ.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

school: Early Muslim and Companion of the Prophet ﷺ who fought at the Battle of Badr (624 CE / 2 AH), the most decisive engagement of early Islam. Died as a martyr (625 CE) at Medina.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Daughter of the Prophet; First to be Buried in Baqi al-Gharqad
full: Ruqayya bint Muhammad ibn Abd Allah al-Hashimiyya
school: Daughter of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and Khadijah bint Khuwaylid. Like her sister Umm Kulthum, she was betrothed before the revelation — to Utba ibn Abi Lahab — and similarly divorced by him when Q111 was revealed. She emigrated to Abyssinia twice with her husband Uthman ibn Affan. She returned to Madinah ill and worsened during the campaign of Badr. She died in Madinah on the very day the news of the victory at Badr reached the city (624 CE / 2 AH); the Prophet was absent at Badr and returned to find her already buried. According to tradition, she was the first Muslim to be buried in Baqi al-Gharqad, the main cemetery of Madinah. Her death prompted the Prophet to say: 'Join our predecessor Uthman ibn Maz'un.' Uthman ibn Affan later married her sister Umm Kulthum.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

school: Son of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, born in Makkah. The third son born to the Prophet; he died in infancy. Also called al-Tayyib and al-Tahir (the Pure). His early death was a source of profound grief for the Prophet.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

school: Son of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and Mariyya al-Qibtiyya (the Coptic), born in Madinah in 630 CE / 9 AH. The Prophet wept deeply at his death in infancy (aged approximately 18 months), saying: 'The eye weeps and the heart grieves, but we say only what pleases our Lord. By Allah, O Ibrahim, we are grieved by your departure.' A solar eclipse occurred on the day of his death; the Prophet clarified that eclipses are signs of Allah, not of human deaths.

books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Foster Mother of the Prophet; Biological Mother of Ali ibn Abi Talib
full: Fatima bint Asad ibn Hashim ibn Abd Manaf al-Hashimiyya
school: Among the most revered women in Islamic tradition — she was both the biological mother of Ali ibn Abi Talib and the foster mother who raised Prophet Muhammad ﷺ after his mother Aminah died when he was six years old. She was the wife of Abu Talib, making her the Prophet's aunt by marriage. She embraced Islam and is counted among the early Companions. The Prophet honoured her with extraordinary devotion throughout his life. When she died (626 CE / 5 AH), the Prophet gave her his own shirt as a burial shroud, prayed her funeral prayer himself — standing four takbirs, the maximum — and lay in her grave before burial so that the grave would be sanctified for her. He said: 'She treated me like her own children, always giving them lesser food so I could eat better.' In Sufi tradition, she holds the highest feminine status as the mother of both the Prophet's rearing and the Imam of walaya. She is buried in Madinah.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Aunt of the Prophet; Poet and Warrior at Uhud
full: Safiyya bint Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim al-Hashimiyya
school: Paternal aunt of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, daughter of Abd al-Muttalib, and full sister of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib. She was among the earliest to embrace Islam and participated in the Battle of Uhud (625 CE) — accounts record her defending the Prophet with a spear when the battle turned against the Muslims, striking at those approaching the Prophet's position. She composed powerful elegies (marathi) for Hamza after his martyrdom at Uhud, which the Prophet heard and wept at. She was also a poet, composing Arabic verse that is preserved in classical sources. She narrated a number of hadith. She is said to have been the first woman to kill an enemy in battle for Islam — according to Ibn Sa'd, she killed a spy who had infiltrated the Muslim encampment at the Trench. She died approximately 641 CE / 20 AH.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Sayyid Banu Hashim — Chief of Banu Hashim; Uncle of the Prophet
full: Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim ibn Abd Manaf al-Qurashi al-Hashimi
dob_s: c. 566 CE (two or three years before the Prophet)
classif: Companion / Uncle of the Prophet / Patriarch of Abbasid Dynasty
titles: Sayyid Banu Hashim · Uncle of the Prophet · Patriarch of the Abbasid Caliphate
school: Paternal uncle of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, approximately two or three years older than his nephew. He was sympathetic to the Prophet's mission from the beginning but publicly accepted Islam late — some accounts say he was already Muslim secretly but maintained his position among the Quraysh to protect Muslims. He was captured at the Battle of Badr fighting on the Quraysh side; the Prophet freed him after he paid a large ransom. He formally embraced Islam at the time of the Conquest of Makkah (630 CE / 8 AH) and thereafter distinguished himself. He participated at the Battle of Hunayn and gave a pivotal rallying call when the Muslims began to flee, shouting out to the companions — his powerful voice turning the rout into a renewed charge. After the Prophet's death, he was one of the most respected figures in the community. He died at age approximately 88 in Madinah. The Abbasid Caliphate (750–1258 CE), which ruled the Islamic world for five centuries, descended from him.

primaryTitle: Brother of Ali ibn Abi Talib; Genealogist of Quraysh
full: Aqeel ibn Abi Talib ibn Abd al-Muttalib al-Hashimi
school: Elder brother of Ali ibn Abi Talib and son of Abu Talib. He was approximately twenty years older than Ali. Known as the greatest genealogist of the Quraysh — the Prophet frequently consulted him on matters of tribal lineage. He embraced Islam after the Conquest of Makkah (630 CE). He participated in the Battle of Hunayn and subsequent campaigns. He was blind in old age and lived during the caliphate of Ali, but maintained a complex relationship with his brother's rule, eventually residing at Muawiyah's court in Damascus due to financial difficulties. He narrated hadith and is a transmitter in various chains. His descendants include many important figures in later Islamic history. He died during the caliphate of Muawiyah (c. 670 CE).

school: Daughter of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib (the Lion of Allah) and therefore a first cousin of the Prophet ﷺ. After her father's martyrdom at Uhud, she was in Makkah. A famous incident occurred: both Ali ibn Abi Talib and Zayd ibn Harithah requested custody of her after Hamza's death. The Prophet awarded her to Jafar ibn Abi Talib, saying: 'The maternal aunt is like the mother.' She is among the female companions.

primaryTitle: Umm Hani bint Abi Talib; Witness to the Night Journey
school: Daughter of Abu Talib and elder sister of Ali ibn Abi Talib, and thus the cousin of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She is notable for two things: the Prophet famously began his Night Journey (Isra) from her house in Makkah according to one narration, and she is recorded in classical sources as having prayed the Fajr prayer with the Prophet after his return, making her a direct witness to the event. When the Makkans besieged her home after the Conquest, she gave sanctuary to two Qurayshi men; the Prophet said: 'We give protection to those you have given protection, Umm Hani.' She refused the Prophet's earlier proposal of marriage (when young) due to her existing marriage. She narrated approximately 46 hadith. She was widowed and lived in Makkah. Her brother Ali and she maintained close ties throughout their lives.
