books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
school: Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ present at the Pledge of Ridwan (Bay'at al-Ridwan) at Hudaybiyya in 628 CE / 6 AH. Allah honoured all who were present by name in the Quran: 'Allah was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree, and He knew what was in their hearts' (Q48:18). Being of Ridwan is one of the highest designations a companion could hold. From the Quraysh tribe of Makkah. Participated in: Khandaq. Known as a scholar of Quranic exegesis (Tafsir). Recognised for scholarship in Islamic jurisprudence (Fiqh). Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Died 693 CE in Makkah.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Companion of the Second Pledge of Aqabah
school: Among the seventy-five men and two women who took the Second Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE / before the Hijra), the founding constitutional moment of the Islamic state in Madinah. The Prophet secretly met this delegation from Madinah at Mina during Hajj and they swore to protect him as they would protect their own families. From the Ansari tribe of Banu salima, Madinah. Recognised for scholarship in Islamic jurisprudence (Fiqh). Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Died 687 CE in Baghdad.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
school: Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who was martyred at Hijaz, Uhud, Uhud. The seventy martyrs of Uhud hold a special place in Islamic memory — the Prophet himself wept over them and they are buried collectively on the battlefield. The Prophet said: 'I am a witness over them.' Died as a martyr (625 CE) at Hijaz, Uhud, Uhud.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Born Muslim — First Generation Born into Islam
school: Among the first generation of Muslims born into the faith — children of early companions who never knew a life before Islam. Their existence was itself a sign of the community's growth and permanence. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Died 663 CE in Medinah.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Greatest Female Poet of Arabic Literature; Mother of Four Martyrs
full: al-Khansa' Tumadur bint Amr ibn al-Harith al-Sulamiyya
titles: Sha'irat al-Arab (Poetess of the Arabs) · Mother of Four Martyrs
school: The greatest female poet in the Arabic literary tradition, widely considered the most gifted elegist (rathiyya poet) in all of Arabic literature, male or female. She composed devastating elegies for her brothers Sakhr and Muawiyah who died in pre-Islamic tribal wars — poems so powerful they set the standard for Arabic grief poetry for centuries. She accepted Islam and came to the Prophet ﷺ, who listened to her poetry and praised it, saying: 'More, Khansa, more!' — she recited standing before him in the mosque. At the Battle of al-Qadisiyya (636 CE), she sent her four sons to fight the Persian forces. When news came that all four had been martyred in a single day, she said: 'Praise be to Allah who honoured me with their martyrdom. I hope Allah will reunite me with them in His mercy.' Her composure in grief stunned the companions. She composed an elegy for all four. She died in Madinah c. 646 CE, the last of the great elegists.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

quranRef: Her sons died at Qadisiyya; their martyrdom is in the tradition of Q3:169

school: Female companion (Sahabiyya) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who narrated hadith and preserved prophetic tradition. From the Quraysh tribe of Makkah. She preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Lived in Makkah.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Umm Atiyya — Most Authoritative Female Voice on Funeral Rites
full: Umm Atiyya Nusayba bint al-Harith al-Ansariyya al-Khazrajiyya
titles: Umm Atiyya · Most cited female authority on funeral and menstrual rulings
school: Ansari companion and one of the most important female narrators of prophetic hadith. She participated in six military campaigns with the Prophet ﷺ, treating the wounded and preparing food — her presence on the battlefield was so valued that she is cited by name in the sources. She is the primary female authority on the Islamic laws of washing and preparing the dead for burial (ghusl al-mayyit); she personally washed the Prophet's daughter Zaynab bint Muhammad at his direction, and he gave her detailed instructions which she preserved and transmitted. Her narrations on menstruation, funeral rites, and women's religious obligations are foundational to Islamic jurisprudence on these topics. She narrated hadith cited in all six major canonical collections. She later lived in Basra where she taught and transmitted hadith until her death.

primaryTitle: Hind bint Utba — The Conversion of Islam's Most Famous Opponent
full: Hind bint Utba ibn Rabi'a ibn Abd Shams al-Umawiyya
school: One of the most dramatic conversion stories in Islamic history. Before Islam she was one of the Prophet's fiercest opponents — she incited the Battle of Uhud, hired Wahshi to kill Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib (the Prophet's uncle), and mutilated Hamza's body after his death, an act she later deeply regretted. She was the wife of Abu Sufyan, leader of Quraysh. She accepted Islam on the day of the Conquest of Makkah (630 CE), coming to the Prophet ﷺ in disguise. When she revealed herself and pledged allegiance, the Prophet accepted her Islam. She thereafter became a sincere Muslim, narrating hadith and participating in the community. She fought at the Battle of Yarmouk (636 CE) against the Byzantine army, rallying the Muslim fighters. She is the mother of Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan. Her story stands as evidence of Islam's willingness to accept any soul, regardless of past hostility.

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
school: Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who was martyred at the Battle of Uhud (625 CE / 3 AH). The seventy martyrs of Uhud hold a special place in Islamic memory — the Prophet himself wept over them and they are buried collectively on the battlefield. The Prophet said: 'I am a witness over them.' Died as a martyr (634 CE) at Hijaz, Yamamah.

books.note: Primary classical source

school: Poet-Companion (Sha'ir al-Rasul) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Poetry was the most powerful medium of public opinion in 7th-century Arabia, and these companions used their talent in defence of Islam, celebrating its victories, mourning its martyrs, and refuting the hostile verse of the Quraysh. The Prophet encouraged them, saying: 'Respond on our behalf — may Jibril support you.'

primaryTitle: Among the First Judges (Qudah) of Islam
full: Attab bin Usayd bin Abi al-'Is
school: Among the first judges (Qudah) appointed in Islam, serving as a Qadhi (judge) during the early Islamic period. Islamic jurisprudence grew out of the practice of these first companions who ruled on disputes according to the Quran and the Prophet's practice, establishing the precedents that later became formal law. From the Quraysh tribe of Makkah. Died 634 CE in Jamadi II, Makkah.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Second Pledge of Aqabah
school: Among the seventy-five men and two women who took the Second Pledge of Aqabah (621 CE / before the Hijra), the founding constitutional moment of the Islamic state in Madinah. The Prophet secretly met this delegation from Madinah at Mina during Hajj and they swore to protect him as they would protect their own families. From the Ansari tribe of Banu salima, Madinah. Participated in: Uhud. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ. Died 674 CE in Syria.

famous: Mushin bin Abi Qays bin al-Aslat
full: Mushin bin Abi Qays bin al-Aslat
school: Poet-Companion (Sha'ir al-Rasul) of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Poetry was the most powerful medium of public opinion in 7th-century Arabia, and these companions used their talent in defence of Islam, celebrating its victories, mourning its martyrs, and refuting the hostile verse of the Quraysh. The Prophet encouraged them, saying: 'Respond on our behalf — may Jibril support you.'

primaryTitle: Companion of the Second Pledge of Aqabah

primaryTitle: Among the First Judges (Qudah) of Islam
school: Among the first judges (Qudah) appointed in Islam, serving as a Qadhi (judge) during the early Islamic period. Islamic jurisprudence grew out of the practice of these first companions who ruled on disputes according to the Quran and the Prophet's practice, establishing the precedents that later became formal law. From the Quraysh tribe of Makkah. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18
school: Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ present at the Pledge of Ridwan (Bay'at al-Ridwan) at Hudaybiyya in 628 CE / 6 AH. Allah honoured all who were present by name in the Quran: 'Allah was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree, and He knew what was in their hearts' (Q48:18). Being of Ridwan is one of the highest designations a companion could hold. From the Quraysh tribe of Makkah. Served as a governor during the early caliphate. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Among the First Judges (Qudah) of Islam
school: Among the first judges (Qudah) appointed in Islam, serving as a Qadhi (judge) during the early Islamic period. Islamic jurisprudence grew out of the practice of these first companions who ruled on disputes according to the Quran and the Prophet's practice, establishing the precedents that later became formal law. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Martyr of the Battle of Uhud
school: Companion of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ who was martyred at Hijaz, Uhud. The seventy martyrs of Uhud hold a special place in Islamic memory — the Prophet himself wept over them and they are buried collectively on the battlefield. The Prophet said: 'I am a witness over them.' Died as a martyr (625 CE) at Hijaz, Uhud.

primaryTitle: Companion of the Pledge of Ridwan — Quran 48:18

Allah honoured all who were present by name in the Quran: 'Allah was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree, and He knew what was in their hearts' (Q48:18). Being of Ridwan is one of the highest designations a companion could hold. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Among the First Judges (Qudah) of Islam
school: Among the first judges (Qudah) appointed in Islam, serving as a Qadhi (judge) during the early Islamic period. Islamic jurisprudence grew out of the practice of these first companions who ruled on disputes according to the Quran and the Prophet's practice, establishing the precedents that later became formal law. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

books.title: Wikipedia — Abu Hazim, Salma bin Dinar
Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Among the First Judges (Qudah) of Islam
school: Among the first judges (Qudah) appointed in Islam, serving as a Qadhi (judge) during the early Islamic period. Islamic jurisprudence grew out of the practice of these first companions who ruled on disputes according to the Quran and the Prophet's practice, establishing the precedents that later became formal law. Preserved and transmitted hadith of the Prophet ﷺ.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

primaryTitle: Mother-in-Law of the Prophet; Most Honoured of Mothers-in-Law
full: Hind bint Awf ibn Zuhayr ibn al-Harith al-Himyariyya
school: Companion from Yemen and the mother-in-law of the Prophet ﷺ — her daughter Maymuna bint al-Harith was the Prophet's last wife. She was also the mother of Umm al-Fadl (wife of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib) and Asma bint Umays (wife of Jafar ibn Abi Talib). The Prophet ﷺ said of her: 'Hind bint Awf is the most honoured of mothers-in-law' — she had three daughters who were all wives of three of the most important men in early Islam simultaneously. She emigrated to Madinah and narrated hadith. She was known for her nobility and character.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Sister of Asma bint Umays; Companion and Narrator
school: Companion (Sahabiyya) and sister of the famous Asma bint Umays (who is in the database). She was the wife of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the Lion of Allah, martyred at Uhud. As Hamza's widow she witnessed the greatest bereavement the early Muslim community suffered after Badr. She later married Shaddad ibn al-Hadi. She narrated hadith from the Prophet ﷺ and preserved several traditions about Hamza and the early community. Her family — the daughters of Umays — were among the most distinguished women in early Islam, with three sisters marrying three of the most important companions.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Umm al-Darda — Greatest Female Scholar of Damascus
full: Umm al-Darda al-Sughra (Hujayma bint Huyayy al-Ansariyya al-Wasabiyya)
titles: Umm al-Darda al-Sughra · Fakhr al-Sham (Pride of Syria)
school: One of the most distinguished female scholars in early Islamic history and the leading female religious authority of Damascus. She studied directly under Abu Darda, the great companion and judge of Damascus (her husband), and under Abu Hurairah. She narrated extensively from the Prophet ﷺ and from the major companions. What makes her exceptional is that she taught in the mosque alongside men — the Caliph Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan himself sat in her teaching circle as a student. She is reported to have said: 'I have sought worship in everything and have found nothing more pleasing to me than sitting with scholars discussing knowledge.' She was consulted on legal rulings by the leading jurists of Syria. Her student Makhul al-Shami — himself a major jurist — transmitted her knowledge. She represents the robust tradition of female scholarship that characterised Damascus in the first Islamic century.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Mother of Aisha; Companion of the Prophet
full: Umm Ruman bint Amir ibn Uwaimir al-Kinaniyya
school: Companion of the Prophet ﷺ and mother of Aisha bint Abi Bakr (Mother of the Believers) and Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Bakr. She was the wife of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. She emigrated with her family to Madinah in the Hijra. She was present during the incident of the Ifk (the slander against Aisha) and witnessed her daughter's distress. When Aisha fell ill during the slander period, Umm Ruman nursed her. The Prophet ﷺ visited her home frequently. She died during the Prophet's lifetime — he descended into her grave himself and prayed for her, saying: 'Whoever wishes to see a woman of the houris (Paradise) let them look at Umm Ruman.' She is buried in Madinah.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: First Woman Appointed to Lead Prayer; Martyr
full: Umm Waraqa bint Abdullah ibn al-Harith al-Ansariyya
school: Ansari companion who memorised the entire Quran (hafiz) during the Prophet's lifetime — rare for anyone, male or female, at that time. She asked the Prophet for permission to go to the Battle of Badr to nurse the wounded; he permitted her. The Prophet ﷺ himself came to her house to visit her and appointed her to lead the prayer for her household — one of the earliest documented cases of a woman being appointed as imam in Islamic history. The Prophet called her 'al-Shahida' (the martyr) before her death, prophesying her fate. She was later murdered by her own servants (who wanted their freedom), making her a martyr as the Prophet had foretold. Her story is cited in discussions of women's religious leadership in classical jurisprudence.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: First or Second Woman to Accept Islam
full: Lubaba al-Kubra bint al-Harith ibn Hazn al-Hilaliyya
school: Also known as Umm al-Fadl, wife of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ. She is considered by many classical scholars to have been the second woman to accept Islam after Khadijah — some place her equal with Khadijah in priority. She narrated directly from the Prophet and her hadith appear in all major canonical collections. She wielded considerable influence in the early community due to her husband's position as the Prophet's uncle and later as the patriarch of the Banu Hashim after the Prophet's death. She was the mother of several important figures including Abdullah ibn Abbas, the greatest Quran scholar among the companions.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

famous: Buzay'a bint Abi Kharija bin Aws
full: Buzay'a bint Abi Kharija bin Aws
school: Ansari companion (Sahabiyya) from Khazraj tribe of Madinah. The Ansar women were the backbone of the early Muslim community in Madinah — they housed the emigrants, donated their jewellery to fund campaigns, nursed the wounded, carried water on the battlefield, and transmitted the Prophet's ﷺ teachings within their households.

books.note: Primary classical source for companion biographies

primaryTitle: Second Woman to Accept Islam; Aunt of Khalid ibn al-Walid
full: Umm al-Fadl Lubaba al-Kubra bint al-Harith ibn Hazn al-Hilaliyya
school: Wife of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib (uncle of the Prophet) and one of the earliest women to accept Islam — according to some accounts, the second woman after Khadijah to enter the faith. She was the maternal aunt of Khalid ibn al-Walid. She narrated several important hadith directly from the Prophet ﷺ, including the definitive ruling on fasting on the Day of Arafat — when people disputed whether the Prophet was fasting, she sent him milk to drink in front of everyone, settling the question. She is also the narrator of a famous hadith in which the Prophet ﷺ showed her how to raise her hands in supplication. Her narrations are in the major canonical collections. She was beloved by the Prophet, who said: 'Umm al-Fadl and Khadijah are the best women.'

school: Ansari companion (Sahabiyya) from Banu al-Najjar of Madinah. The Ansar women were the backbone of the early Muslim community in Madinah — they housed the emigrants, donated their jewellery to fund campaigns, nursed the wounded, carried water on the battlefield, and transmitted the Prophet's ﷺ teachings within their households.

The Muhajirat left behind homes, property and family in Makkah for the sake of their faith — their Hijra is as complete and meritorious as their husbands'. The Quran addresses them directly: 'Their Lord responded to them: I will not let the deeds of any doer among you go to waste, male or female' (Q3:195).

primaryTitle: Zayn al-Abidin — Ornament of the Worshippers; Author of al-Sahifa al-Sajjadiyya
full: Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn Ali ibn Abi Talib al-Hashimi al-Qurashi
classif: Imam / Scholar / Mystic / Son of Husayn ibn Ali
titles: Zayn al-Abidin (Ornament of the Worshippers) · Sayyid al-Sajidin (Master of those who Prostrate) · al-Sajjad (The One Who Prostrates Much) · Abu Muhammad · 4th Imam in Shia tradition
school: Son of Husayn ibn Ali and grandson of Ali ibn Abi Talib — the sole adult male survivor of the massacre of Karbala (680 CE / 61 AH). He was gravely ill during the battle and could not fight; Zaynab bint Ali, his aunt, is credited with shielding him from execution by the forces of Yazid. He was taken as a prisoner to Kufa and then to Damascus, where his composed dignity and the powerful speech of Zaynab turned the tide of public opinion against Yazid. After release he returned to Madinah where he lived the rest of his life in extraordinary piety, scholarship, and ascetic devotion. He authored al-Sahifa al-Sajjadiyya — a collection of 54 supplicatory prayers (du'as) that is considered one of the greatest works of Islamic devotional literature, called 'the Psalms of the House of Muhammad.' His prayers cover every dimension of human spiritual experience — gratitude, repentance, morning and evening supplications, prayers for parents and children — and are recited by millions today across sectarian lines. He spent entire nights in prayer and was seen to weep constantly; when asked why he wept so much he said: 'Jacob wept for one lost son until he lost his sight — I have lost eighteen of my family in a single day.' He transmitted extensive hadith and his students include al-Zuhri and other major Tabi'un scholars. He is the crucial link between Husayn ibn Ali and the continuing Imam tradition, and between the tragedy of Karbala and the living spiritual chain.

books.title: Wikipedia — Ali ibn Husayn Zayn al-Abidin
Al-Sahifa al-Sajjadiyya (The Psalms of Islam)
books.note: His greatest work — 54 supplicatory prayers, called the Psalms of the House of Muhammad

books.title: Risalat al-Huquq (Treatise on Rights)
books.note: The first systematic treatise on human rights in Islamic literature

primaryTitle: Al-Baqir — The Splitter of Knowledge; 5th Imam and Father of Islamic Sciences
full: Muhammad ibn Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn Ali ibn Abi Talib al-Hashimi
classif: Imam / Scholar / Jurist / Son of Zayn al-Abidin
titles: Al-Baqir (Splitter/Disseminator of Knowledge) · Abu Jafar · 5th Imam in Shia tradition · Father of Islamic jurisprudence in the Alid tradition
school: Son of Ali ibn al-Husayn (Zayn al-Abidin) and the teacher of Jafar al-Sadiq — the critical middle link in the chain from Husayn ibn Ali (in this database) to Jafar al-Sadiq (in this database). His title al-Baqir means 'the one who splits open knowledge' — referring to his ability to unlock the inner meanings of the Quran and prophetic tradition. He was present as a child at Karbala (680 CE) and witnessed the massacre. He spent his life in Madinah as the foremost religious authority of the Alid family, teaching an extraordinarily wide circle of students from all schools. His students include Jabir ibn Hayyan (the founder of chemistry who transmitted scientific knowledge from him), major jurists of the Sunni schools, and hundreds of hadith transmitters. He systematised Quranic exegesis (tafsir) and jurisprudence in ways that influenced all subsequent Islamic scholarship — Imam Abu Hanifa reportedly said: 'I have not seen anyone more learned in jurisprudence than Muhammad al-Baqir.' He composed no single famous text but his teachings were recorded by hundreds of students and form the foundation of Jafari jurisprudence. The Jabir ibn Hayyan connection makes him a node between Islamic spiritual and scientific knowledge — his teachings were transmitted into the early development of Islamic chemistry, alchemy, and natural philosophy.

books.title: Ibn Sa'd — Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir
books.note: Primary classical source

teachers: Jabir ibn Abd Allah al-Ansari (last surviving companion)
quranRef: Q33:33 (verse of purification, revealed for his family)
